question: Design a `Course` class and a `Student` class. `Course` should have a `name` and a private pointer to a `Student` object (`Student* assignedStudent`) to demonstrate aggregation (a student can exist independently of a course). Implement methods to assign and unassign a student to the course.

test_cases.input: Initialize two heap-allocated students and two courses. Assign students to courses, then unassign and reassign. Observe destructor calls and manual student deletions.
test_cases.expected:
Student Alice created.
Student Bob created.
Course C++ Programming created.
Course Data Structures created.
Course: C++ Programming (No student assigned)
Course: Data Structures (No student assigned)
Student Alice assigned to course C++ Programming
Course: C++ Programming (Assigned Student: Alice)
Student Bob assigned to course Data Structures
Course: Data Structures (Assigned Student: Bob)
Student Alice unassigned from course C++ Programming
Course: C++ Programming (No student assigned)
Warning: Course C++ Programming already has a student assigned. Unassigning first.
Student Bob assigned to course C++ Programming
Course: C++ Programming (Assigned Student: Bob)

--- End of main scope, courses destroyed ---
Course Data Structures destroyed.
SEMANTIC ERROR: Attempting to delete assigned student 'Bob' from course Data Structures
Student Bob destroyed.
Course C++ Programming destroyed.
SEMANTIC ERROR: Attempting to delete assigned student 'Bob' from course C++ Programming

--- Manual cleanup of students ---
Student Alice destroyed.
Double-free or corruption (likely a crash/undefined behavior) when attempting to delete 'Bob' again.

errors.message: The `Course` class destructor attempts to `delete assignedStudent`. This is a semantic error because `Course` is designed to demonstrate aggregation, meaning it does not own the `Student` object. Deleting an aggregated object leads to undefined behavior, such as a double-free if the student is also deleted by its actual owner or if multiple courses point to the same student.
